figures.gloss: The legendary founder of the yamabushi path: a seventh-century lay ascetic of Mount Katsuragi whom the Shoku Nihongi records as banished to Izu in 699 on a charge of leading people astray with sorcery. Legend gives him command of demon-servants Zenki and Goki, the opening of the Omine peaks, and at last an ascent into the sky as an immortal.
figures.facet: A founder attested first as a criminal — the state's sorcery charge of 699 is the oldest hard fact about him, and everything the tradition reveres grew as legend around that record. Mountain religion's authority here begins outside and against the court's ritual order.
figures.provenance: Shoku Nihongi (entry for 699); Nihon Ryoiki (c. 822, the earliest legend, already calling him a flying immortal); medieval En no Gyoja hagiographies; Miyake Hitoshi's Shugendo scholarship.

figures.gloss: The blue-black wrathful deity said to have burst from the rock of Kinpusen when En no Gyoja prayed for a protector fierce enough for the degenerate age — right leg raised to stamp, vajra brandished. He is a gongen, a 'provisional manifestation': Kinpusen tradition reads him as the composite mountain-form of Shakyamuni, Kannon, and Maitreya (past, present, and future).
figures.facet: A deity native to neither parent religion: not found in continental Buddhist canons, not a classical kami — the clearest single proof that shugen fusion produced something new rather than a mixture. The gongen logic (one buddha-reality wearing local provisional bodies) is compressed into his one figure.
figures.provenance: Kinpusen engi traditions and the Kinpusen-ji cult at Yoshino; the giant Zao triad of Kinpusen-ji's Zaodo; Miyake Hitoshi; Royall Tyler and Paul Swanson's Shugendo studies.

figures.gloss: The practitioner-class of Shugendo: ascetics in the small black tokin cap and suzukake robe who enter the peaks to gain power through ordeal — waterfall austerities, fasting, recitation — and return to serve villages as healers, exorcists, and guides. The conch trumpet (horagai) announces them; in the Edo period tens of thousands were organized into the Honzan-ha (Tendai) and Tozan-ha (Shingon) networks.
figures.facet: The specialist who commutes between wilderness and village — power is won in the mountain but spent in the parish, a professionalized go-between economy of ordeal. The 1872 Meiji ban dissolved the class overnight, making the yamabushi also a study in how a state can amputate a religion by abolishing its personnel.
figures.provenance: H. Byron Earhart, A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo (1970); Miyake Hitoshi, Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion; Meiji Dajokan edict of 1872 abolishing Shugendo.

figures.gloss: The central rite: the ritualized traversal of the Omine range between Yoshino and Kumano, in which the mountain itself is entered as womb and mandala — the route mapped onto the Womb-realm and Diamond-realm mandalas of esoteric Buddhism — and the ascetic passes through the ten realms of rebirth (jikkai shugyo), including being hung out over the cliff-edge to confess.
figures.facet: Doctrine walked instead of read: the mandala is laid over real terrain so that traversing geography IS traversing the cosmos, and the initiate is symbolically gestated and reborn by the landscape. Few traditions anywhere make topography carry this much of the theology.
figures.provenance: The Yoshino-Kumano Okugake-michi practice (still performed; UNESCO-inscribed 2004 within the Kii pilgrimage routes); Miyake Hitoshi; Gorai Shigeru's shugen ethnography.

figures.gloss: The doctrine Shugendo lives inside: kami are the local 'traces' (suijaku) of buddha 'originals' (honji), so mountain, kami, and buddha can be venerated as one continuous sacred order. For roughly a millennium this fusion, not either parent alone, was the normal religion of Japan — until the 1868 shinbutsu bunri decrees tore kami and buddhas apart by law.
figures.facet: A whole civilization's working theology that modern categories erase: because 'Shinto' and 'Buddhism' were legally separated in 1868, the fused thing itself became almost unnameable — and Shugendo, its purest institutional carrier, was banned four years later. The principle survives best in the mountains where it was practiced.
figures.provenance: Medieval honji-suijaku doctrine; Mark Teeuwen and Fabio Rambelli (eds.), Buddhas and Kami in Japan: Honji Suijaku as a Combinatory Paradigm (2003); the 1868 shinbutsu bunri decrees and 1872 Shugendo ban.

figures.gloss: The Immovable Wisdom King — sword in one hand to cut through delusion, rope in the other to bind passions, ringed by a halo of flame — venerated in esoteric Buddhism as one of the Five Wisdom Kings and doctrinally read as a wrathful emanation of the cosmic buddha Dainichi (Mahavairocana).
figures.facet: The yamabushi's working deity of everyday practice, not a distant object of devotion: ascetics ritually identify with Fudo's fierce posture in goma fire rites and mountain austerities, wearing his implacability rather than merely petitioning it.
figures.provenance: Godai Myoo (Five Wisdom Kings) doctrine of Japanese esoteric Buddhism (Shingon/Tendai mikkyo); Miyake Hitoshi's Shugendo scholarship on goma fire ritual.

figures.gloss: The husband-and-wife demon pair that En no Gyoja is said to have subjugated and bound into lifelong service, carrying his belongings and clearing his path through the Omine peaks.
figures.facet: Omine local tradition converts subjugation into descent: the villagers of Dorogawa and Zenkiya are held to be the demons' human descendants, still serving pilgrims on the mountain their ancestors once fought.
figures.provenance: En no Gyoja hagiographic tradition centered on Mount Omine; Dorogawa/Zenkiya village lore as recorded in Miyake Hitoshi's Shugendo scholarship.

figures.gloss: The combined deities of the Kumano Sanzan, the three great shrines of Kumano (Hongu, Shingu, and Nachi), read through honji-suijaku as the local traces of Amida, Yakushi, and Kannon respectively — the goal of the imperial Kumano pilgrimages of the Heian and Kamakura courts.
figures.facet: The southern terminus that completes the Yoshino-Kumano axis: where Zao Gongen anchors the Yoshino end of the Okugake route, Kumano Gongen anchors the other, so that walking the whole mineiri means passing between two independently generated instances of the same gongen logic.
figures.provenance: Kumano Sanzan shrine tradition (Hongu, Shingu, Nachi); the Kii pilgrimage routes (UNESCO-inscribed 2004); Royall Tyler and Paul Swanson's Shugendo studies.

figures.gloss: Shugendo's great outdoor fire ceremony: a monumental pyre of green boughs, ritually built and burned in the open by assembled yamabushi, into which written petitions are cast to Fudo Myoo — the culminating public rite of shugen assemblies.
figures.facet: The one shugen rite performed for a crowd rather than a peak: where the mineiri is a private ordeal of mountain entry, saito goma is Shugendo's most visible and communal ceremony, still regularly staged at temples such as Takao-san and Kinpusen-ji.
figures.provenance: Miyake Hitoshi, Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion; contemporary saito goma practice at Kinpusen-ji and Takao-san.

figures.gloss: The week-long autumn mountain-seclusion at Mt. Haguro of the Dewa Sanzan, in which initiates are ritually gestated through symbolic death and rebirth — the northern counterpart to the Omine okugake and the main surviving initiation cycle outside the Yoshino-Kumano route.
figures.facet: A second, independently surviving proof that Shugendo's core technology is topographic gestation: the same death-and-rebirth structure the Omine route stages through the mandala-mapped Kii mountains recurs at Haguro under a wholly separate lineage and geography.
figures.provenance: H. Byron Earhart, A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo (1970).

figures.gloss: The winged mountain spirits of Japanese folklore, iconographically depicted in yamabushi garb — the small black tokin cap, the pom-pommed robe, the conch trumpet — and by legend the eventual fate of arrogant or fallen ascetics.
figures.facet: Folklore's verdict on the yamabushi's own occupational hazard: dressing tengu as mountain ascetics turns pride in one's austerities into a literal transformation risk, making the tengu less a separate monster than the shugenja's own vanity given a body.
figures.provenance: Japanese folklore traditions of tengu (e.g. Sojobo of Mt. Kurama); iconographic conflation with yamabushi dress documented across Edo-period and earlier sources.

edges.note: Founder and revelation: Zao Gongen is the deity the tradition says En no Gyoja called forth on Kinpusen — the ascetic and his summoned protector define the cult together.
The peak-entry is what makes a yamabushi — rank within the shugen orders is earned by completed mountain-entries; practitioner and rite are one institution.

SHINTO SEAM: under honji-suijaku the sun-goddess herself was identified as the trace of the cosmic buddha Dainichi (Mahavairocana) — Ryobu Shinto mapped Ise's two shrines onto the two mandalas — the paradigm case of the fusion Shugendo inhabits.

BUDDHIST SEAM: the peak-entry transposes Vajrayana's mandala-and-deity technology into landscape — the Omine route is walked as the Womb and Diamond mandalas of Japanese mikkyo, and the ascetic ritually identifies with the wrathful deity (Fudo Myoo) as a yidam practitioner does.
Kinpusen tradition reads Zao Gongen as the composite manifestation of Shakyamuni, Kannon (Avalokitesvara), and Maitreya — the bodhisattva of compassion folded into a mountain god's wrathful body.

TAOIST SEAM: the yamabushi's protective spellcraft carries Daoist talismanic technology — most concretely the nine-syllable kuji formula (rin-pyo-to-sha...), first attested as a protective charm in Ge Hong's Baopuzi, transmitted via onmyodo into shugen practice.

The founder is remembered in the Daoist immortal's vocabulary — the Nihon Ryoiki already has En no Gyoja flying off as a sennin (xian), the transcendent-body ideal that neidan engineers; shared ideal absorbed into his legend, no shared practice claimed.
Honji-suijaku is the one-through-many joint stated as national doctrine: one buddha-reality manifesting through countless local kami vessels.

SHINTO/SHUGENDO SEAM: Hachiman's centuries-long veneration as 'Great Bodhisattva Hachiman' is a paradigm instance, alongside Zao Gongen and Amaterasu-as-Dainichi, of the honji-suijaku fusion Shugendo institutionalizes.
Intra-tradition: the founder's subjugation of the demon couple is the signature motif of his hagiography, still localized at Dorogawa/Zenkiya.
Intra-tradition: Fudo is the yamabushi's principal object of devotion and self-identification in goma fire rites and mountain austerities.
Intra-tradition: the great outdoor fire rite culminates in offerings and written petitions burned to Fudo Myoo.
Intra-tradition: the two termini of the Okugake pilgrimage — Yoshino (Zao Gongen) and Kumano (Kumano Gongen) — together define the mineiri route.
Intra-tradition: Kumano is the southern terminus of the peak-entry route the mineiri entry describes.

BUDDHIST SEAM: under honji-suijaku, the Nachi shrine of the Kumano triad is specifically identified with Kannon (Avalokitesvara) — the same bodhisattva already folded into Zao Gongen's composite identity.
The Dewa Sanzan's autumn peak-retreat and the Yoshino-Kumano Okugake are independently attested lineages sharing the same death-and-rebirth structure of mountain-entry initiation.
Iconographic conflation: tengu are depicted in full yamabushi dress, and legend casts prideful ascetics as becoming tengu — folklore's own commentary on the practitioner class.

dossier.essence: The mountain-ascetic tradition of Japan: a fusion of kami worship, esoteric (Tendai and Shingon) Buddhism, Daoist spellcraft, and shamanic practice, organized around gaining power through ordeal in the sacred peaks. Its practitioners, the yamabushi, trace themselves to the seventh-century ascetic En no Gyoja; the tradition was banned outright by Meiji decree in 1872 and legally revived after 1945, and peak-entries are still walked at Yoshino-Kumano, Mount Haguro, and elsewhere.
dossier.cosmos: Shugendo lives inside the honji-suijaku worldview: kami are the local 'traces' of buddha 'originals,' so mountain, kami, and buddha form one continuous sacred order rather than two religions. Specific mountains are read as mandalas made of terrain — the Omine range is traversed as the Womb and Diamond realms of esoteric Buddhism — and as zones where the ten realms of rebirth, hells included, can be passed through in this body. The ascetic who completes the traversal is held to be symbolically gestated and reborn by the mountain itself, achieving buddhahood in this very body (sokushin jobutsu) rather than after death.
dossier.practice: The core rite is mineiri, the disciplined peak-entry: guided ascents with fasting, waterfall austerities, recitation, fire ceremonies (saito goma), and ordeal-stations such as being hung out over a cliff-edge to confess. Power won in the mountain is spent in the village — yamabushi historically served parishes as healers, exorcists, diviners, and pilgrimage guides, tens of thousands of them organized in the Edo period into the Tendai-affiliated Honzan-ha and Shingon-affiliated Tozan-ha networks. The 1872 ban dissolved this professional class overnight; today's practice centers on temple-led seasonal peak-entries open to lay participants.
dossier.canon: No closed scripture of its own: the tradition draws on the Lotus Sutra and esoteric Buddhist ritual corpus, its own shugen liturgical manuals and mountain foundation-legends (engi), and hagiographies of En no Gyoja — whose oldest attestations are the Shoku Nihongi entry for 699 and the Nihon Ryoiki (c. 822).
dossier.reflects: Sacred power as territorial and bodily — doctrine walked rather than read, with real topography made to carry the cosmology and ordeal made the credential of religious authority.
sources: H. Byron Earhart, 'A Religious Study of the Mount Haguro Sect of Shugendo' (1970)
Miyake Hitoshi, 'Shugendo: Essays on the Structure of Japanese Folk Religion' (2001)
Mark Teeuwen & Fabio Rambelli (eds.), 'Buddhas and Kami in Japan: Honji Suijaku as a Combinatory Paradigm' (2003)
Carmen Blacker, 'The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan' (1975)
